g.data-narration: Welcome to JSP Variables and Objects. This lecture covers declaration, scopes, and implicit objects in Java Server Pages.
g.text: Java Server Pages | Dynamic Web Development

g.data-narration: JSP enables dynamic web content by combining HTML with Java code. It simplifies servlet programming through special tags and implicit objects.
g.text: JSP is a server-side technology that simplifies creating dynamic web content.
Unlike plain HTML, JSP allows embedding <% Java code %> directly in pages.
JSPs are translated to servlets before execution by the servlet container.

g.data-narration: JSP provides special variable types: scriptlet variables for local logic, declaration variables for class members, and nine built-in implicit objects.
Scriptlet variables exist within the _jspService method. They're local to each request and reinitialized on every page access.
g.text: Declared inside <% ... %> tags. These become local variables in the _jspService() method.

g.data-narration: Declaration variables use the exclamation mark syntax. They become instance variables of the generated servlet class and persist across requests.
g.text: Declared inside <%! ... %> tags. These become instance variables of the generated servlet class.

g.data-narration: JSP offers four scopes: page for current page only, request for same HTTP request chain, session for user sessions, and application for all users.
g.text: findAttribute() searches: page → request → session → application

g.data-narration: JSP provides nine automatic objects: request, response, session, application, out, config, page, pageContext, and exception. Each serves a specific purpose.
The request object represents the HTTP request from the client. It provides methods to access parameters, headers, cookies, and request dispatching.
g.text: Handles client request data including parameters, headers, and cookies.

g.data-narration: The response object controls what gets sent back to the client. Use it to set content type, add cookies, and redirect users to other pages.
g.text: Controls the HTTP response sent back to the client.

g.data-narration: The session object maintains user state across multiple requests. Store user-specific data like login status, shopping cart, and preferences.
g.text: Maintains user-specific state across multiple requests from the same client.

g.data-narration: The application object represents the entire web application. It shares data across all users and persists for the app's lifetime.
g.text: Represents the entire web application. Data is shared across all users and sessions.

g.data-narration: Other implicit objects include: out for output, config for servlet config, page for the servlet reference, and pageContext for managing scopes.
The exception object is only available in error pages. It provides access to the thrown exception for displaying error messages.
g.text: Available only in error pages (isErrorPage="true"). Used for exception handling.

g.data-narration: Cookies store small data on the client browser. Use response to add cookies and request to retrieve them. Set maxAge for persistence.
g.text: • setMaxAge(): Positive = persistent, 0 = delete, Negative = session cookie

g.data-narration: This login example demonstrates form handling with request parameters and session management for user authentication.
This counter shows both session-scoped and application-scoped variables. Synchronize the global counter for thread safety.
Key points: Use declarations for instance variables, scriptlets for local variables. Four scopes determine lifetime. Nine implicit objects simplify development.
g.text: • <%! %> for class-level variables, <% %> for local variables
• Four scopes: page < request < session < application
• Nine implicit objects: request, response, session, application, out, config, page, pageContext, exception

g.data-narration: Remember: findAttribute searches page to application. Declaration variables need synchronization. Parameters are read-only, attributes are settable.
g.text: • findAttribute() search order: page → request → session → application
• Declaration vars need synchronized for thread safety
